records.sentence: Recurring-service commitment (first-month discount + upfront fee) and free-trial-with-penalty both use financial friction to signal sunk-cost and drive continuation; the second layers urgency on top of the first's commitment mechanism.
Sales speed and first-contact advantage are orthogonal to free-trial friction; both increase conversion, but through different mechanisms (responsiveness vs. commitment barrier).
Framing close as permission and collecting deposit on-site are tactical closes; the free-trial-with-penalty model operationalizes urgency at the product level, making the closer's job easier.
Webinar soft-offer builds trust without friction; free-trial-with-penalty adds friction to accelerate commitment—opposite trust strategies targeting the same outcome (conversion).
Funnel-page friction and free-trial-penalty friction both compress conversion; but funnel optimization targets messaging and layout clarity while free-trial targets psychological commitment barriers.
Premium niche pricing and price-as-signal-of-quality both assert that higher price increases customer perception and commitment; niche positioning amplifies this effect by concentrating price power.
Tiered pricing stacks multiple price points; price-as-signal asserts that the premium tier's price anchors expectations upward, making the tier structure executable.
Free-to-paid funnel uses decoy pricing and anchoring to position paid tiers as 10x more valuable; price-as-signal explains the psychological mechanism enabling that positioning.
Sales targeting uses qualification friction to raise close rates; price-as-signal raises customer expectations and commitment—both increase conversion but through different customer-stage levers.
Brand-as-moat enables premium pricing; price-as-signal explains why customers accept that premium (belief alignment compounds willingness-to-pay).
Identity construction reorients from external validation to internal values; reverse-eulogy surfaces which worries are noise—both address founder clarity, but at different time horizons.
Offer pruning eliminates low-ROI volume; reverse-eulogy identifies which values matter—when applied to business offers, eulogy surfaces which offers to prune.
Marketing clarity over production prioritizes message over polish; reverse-eulogy clarifies what truly matters—both emphasize stripping distractions, but one targets content and one targets values.
Focus-through-subtraction scales by deepening core strength; reverse-eulogy identifies which strengths are actually aligned with founder values—eulogy arms the subtraction decision.
Behavior-change framing positions directives as observable changes; reverse-eulogy surfaces value-aligned behavior—eulogy provides the values justifying which behaviors to frame.
Conscious-trade accounting identifies unconscious costs; reverse-eulogy exposes which costs are noise vs. signal—both surface hidden decision structure.
Premium-positioning on defensible attributes and reverse-eulogy both justify rejecting price competition; eulogy clarifies which defensibility matters to founder values.
Pre-processing-complexity monetization hides work upstream; reverse-eulogy clarifies whether absorbing that complexity aligns with founder values and mission.
Value-bifurcation separates skill-building from consumables; reverse-eulogy identifies which customer value matters most—eulogy arms the bifurcation design.
Identity-as-aggregate-of-actions treats behavior as malleable; context-as-judgment-dissolvent explains why: tracing reinforcement history removes moral judgment, enabling behavioral change.
Failure-as-recoverable-iteration reframes failure as solvable; context-as-dissolvent explains the mechanism: understanding constraints removes the emotional brake on iteration.
Trust earned through consistent reinforcement and context-as-dissolvent both argue that behavior drives outcomes—but trust is about others' beliefs while context is about self-judgment.
Behavioral-reinforcement-loops and context-as-dissolvent both map how repeated consequences shape identity and action; context is the mechanism behind why certain reinforcements stick.
Cold-start trust via aligned incentives and context-as-dissolvent both remove interpersonal friction; understanding context removes judgment and accelerates trust.
Resilience-as-recovery-speed and context-as-dissolvent both reduce setback impact; but resilience is about speed of rebound while context is about understanding why setback occurred.
Narrative-motivation ads and context-as-dissolvent both appeal to identity-based reasoning; but ads are about projected identity while context removes identity barriers to action.
Referral-economics and context-as-dissolvent both argue repeated touchpoints increase desired outcomes; but referral tracks economic incentives while context is about understanding.
Opportunity-cost framework surfaces invisible tradeoffs; context-as-dissolvent explains why tradeoffs cause regret (unknown costs feel larger)—both argue for explicit mapping.
Founder-resilience-narrative describes loss-shaped psychology; context-as-dissolvent explains why: mapping that reinforcement history removes judgment and enables forward action.
Profit-share-alignment and contingent-refund both tie financial skin-in-the-game to performance outcomes; profit-share applies to operations while refund applies to customer commitment.
Deal-structure-and-ownership governs equity allocation across batch; profit-share applies that structure to individual-operator-level incentives—both prevent misalignment.
Founder-to-trainer-multiplier transition scales delivery; profit-share-alignment enables that transition by tying GM compensation to location performance instead of founder oversight.
Activity-based-time-valuation delegates below founder's threshold rate; profit-share enables delegation by aligning operator incentives without founder micromanagement.
Leverage-mechanics multiplies key input actions; profit-share-alignment multiplies those actions by enabling independent operator decision-making.
Cross-platform retargeting multiplies touchpoints; multi-touch-attribution-ambiguity explains why: with 5-10 channels, credit allocation becomes structurally unclear.
Distribution-layer-strategy and multi-touch-attribution-ambiguity both address multi-channel acquisition; but distribution builds recurring relationships while attribution addresses measurement.
Volume-first-scaling and multi-touch-attribution-ambiguity both operate across channels; but volume ignores attribution while attribution explains why volume compounds.
Customer-awareness-levels and multi-touch-attribution-ambiguity both address funnel dynamics across stages; but awareness targets messaging while attribution addresses channel credit.
Multi-touch-attribution-ambiguity names the measurement problem; cumulative-secondary-touchpoints-compound asserts that secondary touches matter more than primary-channel credit matters.
Sales-targeting-and-qualification and multi-touch-attribution-ambiguity both operate on prospect quality; but targeting filters pre-funnel while attribution measures post-click.
Long-term-horizon advantage and multi-touch-attribution-ambiguity both address multi-year customer journeys; long-term outlook enables accepting ambiguous attribution.
Supply-vs-demand-diagnosis and multi-touch-attribution-ambiguity both diagnose growth blockers; but one targets capacity while the other targets measurement clarity.
Content-surface-area and multi-touch-attribution-ambiguity both scale touchpoints; but surface-area targets content volume while attribution addresses multi-touch credit.
Ad-spend-optimization (raise quality before raise budget) and paid-plus-organic-entry both optimize for CAC preservation; optimization at creative level enables low-budget paid path.
Business-model-bifurcation asserts mid-market is untenable; paid-plus-organic-parallel asserts new-market entry requires both channels—neither alone works at required timeline.
Free-to-paid-funnel-architecture and paid-plus-organic-parallel both operate dual channels; but free-to-paid is funnel structure while paid-plus-organic is go-to-market simultaneity.
Content-led-sales-strategy and paid-plus-organic-parallel both assert winners in organic become scalable via paid; they describe the same playbook from two angles.
Payment-timing-optimization and paid-plus-organic-parallel both address cash-flow constraints in scaling; but timing optimizes daily working capital while organic eliminates ad-spend constraint.
Attention-to-revenue-funnel and paid-plus-organic-parallel both describe pathways from awareness to monetization; parallel-entry operationalizes the funnel across dual channels.
Scaling-performance-marketing-compresses-margins and paid-plus-organic-parallel both name growth ceilings in paid channels; organic provides the alternative leg to breakeven.
Revenue-mix-recurring-paid-organic and paid-plus-organic-parallel both allocate focus across channels; parallel-entry describes the sequencing for new markets.
Tiered-offer-pricing and paid-plus-organic-parallel both address monetization; but tiers optimize offer stack while parallel-entry optimizes channel mix.
Paid-plus-organic-parallel and guided-A/B-menu-upsell both increase attach rate; parallel-entry increases customer acquisition while menu-structure increases per-customer revenue.
Capital-requirements-for-scale and paid-plus-organic-parallel both assert that high-end markets are bootstrappable; organic path eliminates capital constraint in new markets.
Attention-to-revenue-funnel and client-financed-acquisition-loop both describe pathways from attention to monetization; high-ticket offers at top of funnel generate the capital for reinvestment.
Niche-down-to-scale-up and client-financed-acquisition-loop both assert that premium niche pricing generates capital for growth; loop operationalizes the reinvestment.
Pay-less-now-upsell-architecture and client-financed-acquisition-loop both monetize high-ticket offers; upsell stacks front offer and back offer, while loop reinvests combined revenue into ads.
Capital-deployment-paths and client-financed-acquisition-loop both address accumulated capital allocation; loop is the reinvestment-into-core path that avoids external capital needs.
Recurring-revenue-valuation-premium and client-financed-acquisition-loop both explain capital generation; recurring models command multiples that enable higher reinvestment capacity.
Tiered-offer-pricing and client-financed-acquisition-loop both stack offers; high-margin premium tiers generate the immediate profit that funds paid-ad reinvestment.

Value-based-pricing-and-margin-optimization and guided-A/B-menu-upsell both prescribe offering choice; A/B structure operationalizes value-based pricing by guiding customers toward margin-optimal option.
Sales-targeting and guided-A/B-menu-upsell both increase close rates; targeting filters who closes while A/B structure ensures high-quality-customers close at premium-tier attach.
Attention-to-revenue-funnel and guided-A/B-menu-upsell both monetize attention via sequenced offers; A/B-menu is the offer-stage implementation of the funnel architecture.
Sales-closing-mechanics and guided-A/B-menu-upsell both operationalize close-rate improvement; prescribing-what-customer-needs and framing-close-as-permission both make closing frictionless.
Tiered-offer-pricing and guided-A/B-menu-upsell both present multiple price points; A/B-menu is the implementation pattern for tiered pricing at the point of sale.
Upwork-profile-optimization targets freelance positioning; acquisition-only-vs-done-for-you addresses business-model positioning—both optimize buyer perception.
Pay-less-now-upsell-architecture removes front-offer friction; acquisition-only-vs-done-for-you asserts that pure-acquisition positioning is preferable when operationalization costs exceed upside.
Guided-A/B-menu-upsell and acquisition-only-vs-done-for-you both address offer structure; menu optimizes attach while positioning optimizes operationalization.
Offer-and-audience-pruning and acquisition-only-vs-done-for-you both prune low-ROI offerings; done-for-you removal is a specific pruning decision based on operationalization cost.

Distribution-layer-strategy and acquisition-only-vs-done-for-you both address customer acquisition models; but distribution builds recurring relationships while acquisition-only targets transactional leverage.
Acquisition-only positioning reduces client friction by removing operationalization burden; contingent-refund adds financial accountability to ensure execution—both maximize completion.
Attention-to-revenue-funnel and cumulative-secondary-touchpoints both describe multi-stage conversion; secondary-touchpoints explain why frequency inside the funnel compounds conversion.

Distribution-layer-strategy and cumulative-secondary-touchpoints both compound relationships over time; but distribution builds recurring-referral-pipelines while secondary-touchpoints optimize short-cycle conversion.
Referral-and-retention-economics and cumulative-secondary-touchpoints both address repeat-customer touch dynamics; economics optimizes incentives while secondary-touchpoints optimize timing.
Member-retention-follow-up applies personal outreach to prevent churn; cumulative-secondary-touchpoints explains why outreach works—secondary-touch accumulated over 30 days drives completion.
Uncertainty-tolerance bounds potential as a ceiling; burnout-as-trainable-capacity asserts capacity grows with volume—one treats potential as fixed while the other treats it as trainable.
Founder-bottleneck names the capacity constraint; burnout-as-trainable-capacity reframes that constraint as resolvable through training rather than structural limitation.
Content-volume-as-moat and burnout-as-trainable-capacity both argue sustained high-output is possible; moat claims output compounds while capacity claims volume increases ability.
Volume-and-repetition-mastery and burnout-as-trainable-capacity both assert that high-volume deliberate action improves performance; both describe training-through-repetition.
Control-paradox and burnout-as-trainable-capacity both address growth barriers; but control is about mindset unlearning while burnout is about physical capacity expansion.
Recruiting-high-leverage-talent and burnout-as-trainable-capacity both address scaling constraints; but recruiting solves the binding constraint while capacity training increases founder's ceiling.
Sales-targeting and contingent-refund both increase commitment and completion; targeting filters high-intent buyers while contingent-refund adds financial accountability for completion.
Continuity-upsell and contingent-refund both leverage completion momentum; upsell capitalizes on proof while contingent-refund ensures that proof is earned.
Attention-to-revenue-funnel and contingent-refund both monetize attention and commitment; contingent-refund is the monetization-with-accountability stage inside the funnel.

Recurring-service-pricing-structure and contingent-refund both use upfront commitment to reduce churn; but recurring pricing structures ongoing payments while contingent-refund ties refund to execution.
Cold-start-trust-architecture and proof-promise-plan-video both address low-trust contexts; video operationalizes trust-building through credibility (proof), value (promise), and clarity (plan).
Offer-value-architecture and proof-promise-plan-video both maximize perceived value; architecture designs the offer while video frames it—both layer credibility into the first impression.
Founder-authenticity-and-audience-alignment and proof-promise-plan-video both build trust via creator credibility; video structure operationalizes alignment by establishing proof early.
Audience-building-and-call-out-mechanics and proof-promise-plan-video both create attention leverage; video framework provides the credibility structure that makes call-out mechanics credible.
Content-offer-congruence ensures audience alignment; proof-promise-plan-video ensures offer-pitch congruence—both prevent misalignment between audience expectation and delivery.
Pre-call-signal-stacking layers credibility; proof-promise-plan-video front-loads that stacking in the first impression, making pre-call-stacking redundant when video is strong.
Webinar-soft-offer and proof-promise-plan-video both build trust without hard close; webinar is a longer format while video is initial introduction—both follow the proof-promise pattern.
Early-stage-identity-construction and proof-promise-plan-video both establish founder credibility early; identity is internal alignment while video is external proof.
Social-proof-hierarchy and proof-promise-plan-video both build credibility; video framework provides the context where testimonials (highest proof) create maximum impact.
Attention-to-revenue-funnel and proof-promise-plan-video both convert attention to commitment; video is the first-stage trust-builder that gates entry into the funnel.
Content-led-sales-strategy and first-party-data-pattern-extraction both use proprietary-content success to drive scaling; extraction systematizes the pattern-recognition behind content winners.
Attention-to-revenue-funnel and first-party-data-pattern-extraction both optimize pathways at scale; data extraction identifies which funnel-stage patterns recur across customers.

Sales-targeting-and-qualification and first-party-data-pattern-extraction both filter high-intent buyers; targeting uses demographic-intent signals while data-extraction uses behavioral-conversion patterns.
Single-channel-domination and first-party-data-pattern-extraction both build compounding advantage; domination concentrates data on one channel, which extraction then systematizes.

Sales-speed-and-first-contact-advantage and first-party-data-pattern-extraction both apply velocity optimization; but speed is tactical while data-extraction identifies whether speed patterns vary by signal.
Ad-creative-assembly-and-variation and first-party-data-pattern-extraction both scale creative output; extraction identifies which variation patterns win most, enabling template optimization.
Classic-upsell and first-party-data-pattern-extraction both leverage existing customer data; upsell applies to next-problem discovery while extraction identifies which patterns predict willingness.

Focus-through-subtraction warns against spreading founder across business models; founder-delegation-sequence explains the sequencing error (GM + training replacement simultaneously) as the inverse of proper subtraction.
Focus-through-subtraction is the strategic choice of which constraint to solve; constraint-identification is the diagnostic that enables that choice.
Focus-through-subtraction argues for deepening core strength; talent-acquisition-levels operationalizes that deepening by prioritizing highest-damage-role first.

Focus-through-subtraction and brand-attribution-window both warn against optimization patterns that erode business model; but subtraction is structural (removing offers) while attribution-window is methodological (removing brand).
Focus-through-subtraction argues for narrowing business model; single-channel-domination operationalizes that narrowing at the acquisition level.
Focus-through-subtraction warns against multi-offer distraction; licensing-model describes the specific case where that distraction proved uncertain for Hormozi.
Focus-through-subtraction argues business-model spread slows core; unit-economics-and-profit-focus asserts the same principle applies to unit structure—both warn against dilution.
Focus-through-subtraction opposes spreading to adjacent models; supply-constraint-recognition argues that operations constraint should be solved before course launch—both oppose distraction.

Sales-process-optimization and supply-constraint-levers both prescribe sequencing: demand-constraint path (fix-sales-then-raise-price) mirrors supply-constraint path (raise-price-then-hire), just with constraints flipped.
Sales-process-optimization (demand-constrained) and supply-constraint-recognition (operationally-constrained) both prescribe sequencing for their respective constraint types.
Sales-process-optimization targets the sales funnel; activation-as-churn-lever targets the onboarding handoff—both address the early-stage funnel stages that determine downstream results.
Sales-process-optimization asserts sales-funnel fixes depend on upstream factors; offer-value-architecture names those upstream factors explicitly.
Sales-process-optimization (demand-constrained) and funnel-optimization both prescribe diagnostic-first sequencing before applying tactics.

Sales-process-optimization and service-business-scaling-strategy both address service-business growth; but optimization targets demand-constrained path while scaling-strategy targets automation-constrained path.
Sales-process-optimization generates cash flow from sales; payment-timing-optimization applies that cash flow to capital allocation.
Sales-process-optimization targets the sales motion; promise-delivery-gap targets the fulfillment motion—both are upstream operations that determine churn.

Founder-bottleneck and delivery-capacity-bottleneck both assert that delivery capacity constraints growth; founder-bottleneck names the person while capacity-bottleneck names the structural constraint.
Founder-bottleneck identifies the constraint; founder-removal-test operationalizes the constraint resolution by testing whether founder extraction enables replication.
Founder-bottleneck and peer-clinician-hiring both address founder-delivery constraints; but founder-bottleneck is general while peer-clinician is domain-specific implementation.
Founder-bottleneck identifies delivery constraint; growth-path-selection explains the choice: face-driven services are harder to replicate than corporate-brand services.
Founder-bottleneck names the capacity constraint; single-unit-mastery-before-replication operationalizes the solution by requiring demand-exceeds-supply before replication attempt.
Founder-bottleneck and supply-constraint-recognition both assert delivery capacity limits growth; supply-constraint operationalizes the path (offshore-leverage, data-architecture, hiring).
Founder-bottleneck identifies delivery constraint; channel-optimization-before-expansion warns against expanding channels when replication is not yet proven.
Founder-bottleneck and talent-acquisition-levels both address capacity; but founder-bottleneck is the delivery constraint while talent-acquisition is the hiring-sequencing solution.

Supply-vs-demand-constraint-diagnosis and single-unit-mastery-before-replication both establish the diagnostic: supply-constrained means demand exceeds supply, which means you've achieved mastery readiness.
Supply-vs-demand-constraint-diagnosis and delivery-capacity-bottleneck both name the supply-constraint problem; supply-constraint-diagnosis explains why it exists.
Supply-vs-demand-constraint-diagnosis asserts most service businesses are supply-constrained; supply-constraint-recognition explains why (people-finding > product-finding).
Supply-vs-demand-constraint-diagnosis applies to service businesses; e-commerce-business-model-economics describes the opposite constraint set (capital + supply-chain + distribution).
Supply-vs-demand-constraint-diagnosis identifies the constraint type; supply-constraint-levers prescribes the sequencing for supply-constrained cases.
Supply-vs-demand-constraint-diagnosis and service-business-scaling-strategy both assert service businesses should move upmarket; upmarket positioning solves supply constraint by reducing volume.
Supply-vs-demand-constraint-diagnosis and offer-value-architecture both address upstream funnel issues; diagnosis identifies supply vs. demand while architecture identifies offer-structure issues.
Supply-vs-demand-constraint-diagnosis is a specific application of constraint-identification framework.

Revenue-stage-transitions (narrow + productize at $1-3M) and unit-economics-and-profit-focus both identify arbitrage as the business core; stage-transitions prescribe the specific sequencing for $1-3M.
Revenue-stage-transitions (separate tiers and offer architecture) and continuity-upsell both build recurring revenue; stage-transitions targets the $1-3M sequencing while upsell adds the back-offer.

Revenue-stage-transitions (narrowing to solve constraint) and delivery-capacity-bottleneck both address $1-3M businesses; but stage-transitions targets market-side constraints while bottleneck targets delivery-side.
Revenue-stage-transitions and premium-positioning both address $1-3M scaling; but stage-transitions prescribes LTV-to-CAC narrowing while positioning prescribes volume-of-dollars strategy.
Revenue-stage-transitions targets highest-LTV-to-CAC avatar; LTV-to-CAC-measurement-timeline describes the measuring and monitoring of that ratio.
Revenue-stage-transitions (narrow to high-LTV avatar) and high-value-customers-are-less-demanding both assert that serving fewer high-value customers is operationally easier and more scalable.
Revenue-stage-transitions and scaling-bottleneck-diagnosis both address scaling ceilings; transitions prescribe the $1-3M sequence while diagnosis identifies where $10M breakeven occurs.

Revenue-stage-transitions (pursue highest LTV-to-CAC) and psychological-barriers-to-growth both address founder decision-making at $1-3M; transitions target market selection while barriers address margin obsession.
Tradeoffs-and-conscious-sacrifice frames delivery-model transition as tradeoff; churn-and-retention asserts the same outcome—departing clients make room for new higher-paying clients.
Tradeoffs-and-conscious-sacrifice (short-term loss for permanent gain) and continuity-upsell both operationalize the transition by separating high-value-upfront from ongoing-low-value tiers.

Tradeoffs-and-conscious-sacrifice and founder-character-and-team-attraction both address founder decision-making at scale; sacrifice identifies the tradeoff while character identifies hiring implications.

Tradeoffs-and-conscious-sacrifice and control-paradox both assert that founder must unlearn old skills to scale; sacrifice is the strategic tradeoff mindset while control-paradox is the specific skill to unlearn.
Tradeoffs-and-conscious-sacrifice (accept short-term loss for long-term gain) and distinctiveness-requires-active-effort both argue that tactical concessions enable strategic advantage.

Tradeoffs-and-conscious-sacrifice and distribution-layer-strategy both address customer-transition: sacrifice accepts short-term loss while distribution-strategy replaces lost customers with distributor relationships.
Tradeoffs-and-conscious-sacrifice addresses macro-level decisions; systemic-vs-segment-risk-diagnosis applies that thinking to revenue-decline situations—identify whether loss is systemic or local.
Tradeoffs-and-conscious-sacrifice and control-paradox both frame founder integrity: continuing unsustainable model out of fear vs. choosing the hard tradeoff that serves stated mission.
Avatar-targeting-strategy and continuity-upsell both target high-activation avatars; strategy identifies which avatar segments convert while upsell structures their offer-journey.
Avatar-targeting-strategy diagnoses high-churn by segment; systemic-vs-segment-risk-diagnosis applies that segmentation to identify whether problem is universal or local.
Avatar-targeting-strategy and channel-optimization-before-expansion both warn against premature pivoting; strategy says validate avatar-profitability first before channel expansion.
Avatar-targeting-strategy targets high-activation avatars; activation-as-churn-lever explains why—fixing expectation-misalignment in onboarding increases activation within any avatar.
Avatar-targeting-strategy targets which customer to close; sales-team-structure targets how-to-close for each channel—both address sales-force optimization.
Avatar-targeting-strategy identifies high-activation avatar; setup-fee-as-churn-reduction operationalizes retention within that avatar by adding commitment-friction.
Avatar-targeting-strategy and service-business-scaling-strategy both assert that service businesses should pursue best-customers-at-premium-price rather than volume-at-low-price.
Avatar-targeting-strategy (replace low-activation with high-activation clients) and focus-through-subtraction both argue for replacing rather than scaling existing model.
Premium-positioning and pay-less-now-upsell both address pricing psychology; but positioning drives emotional investment while upsell removes front-offer friction.
Premium-positioning and differentiation-and-premium-positioning both assert premium pricing works when value exceeds price; high-retention (95%+) is the signal that positioning is validated.
Premium-positioning (higher price increases commitment and results) and setup-fee-as-churn-reduction both use financial commitment to improve outcomes—sunk-cost reduces churn.
Premium-positioning and wealth-distribution-and-customer-targeting both assert that most businesses under-price for the customers with money; targeting the wealthy enables premium positioning.
Premium-positioning creates perceived value; value-based-pricing-and-margin-optimization operationalizes that value by anchoring high and hiding cost—both maximize price realization.
Premium-positioning and motivated-buyer-targeting both address willingness-to-pay; but positioning creates investment while targeting selects customers already willing.
Premium-positioning asserts premium pricing drives results and commitment; pareto-pricing-four-tier applies premium positioning at multiple tiers (hobby, pro, L1, L2, L3).
Premium-positioning and downsell-tier-creation both operationalize multi-tier strategy; downsell paradoxically increases premium take-rate by providing contrast.
Hiring-as-capital-allocation and sales-compensation-benchmarks both frame hiring as ROI calculation—salesperson investment is evaluated by gross-profit-per-salesperson.

Hiring-as-capital-allocation and intermediary-as-primary-customer both apply ROI logic; hiring an AE targets end-customers while hiring CPA relationships targets intermediaries—same capital-allocation logic.
